summary: Pulmonary hypertension significantly affects survival in patients with congenital diaphragmatic hernia; managing it effectively improves patient outcome. This condition represents one of the primary determinants for predicting long-term success or failure after treatment due to its strong correlation with mortality risk factors associated with severe lung underdevelopment seen in such cases. Agent1 identified pulmonary hypertension as the paramount predictor because multiple studies highlight its substantial impact on both short-term viability through mechanical ventilation support via extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO) if necessary, along with longer term survivability metrics tied directly back towards controlling pressures within lungs post-surgery when addressing defects caused prenatally during fetal development stages leading up birth itself where proper function must resume immediately thereafter without delay otherwise risking permanent damage not fully reversible even given best available care options currently known today regarding neonatal intensive unit interventions designed explicitly around supporting compromised organ systems including but certainly not limited only heart/lungs among others potentially involved depending upon specific nature/extent/severity level present at time point diagnosis made clinically observable externally/internal examination performed internally using imaging modalities etc...
